title_en: Ibn Hajar al-Asqalani: Life and Scholarly Formation
title_ar: ابن حجر العسقلاني: حياته وتكوينه العلمي
content_en:
Ahmad ibn Ali ibn Muhammad ibn Muhammad ibn Ali ibn Ahmad, known as Ibn Hajar al-Asqalani, was born in Cairo in 773 AH (1372 CE). His family originated from the coastal town of Asqalan (Ashkelon) in Palestine, though he himself was born and spent most of his life in Egypt. Orphaned at an early age, he was raised and educated by guardians who recognized his exceptional intellectual gifts. He began studying the Quran and Islamic sciences as a child and quickly distinguished himself through his extraordinary memory and his capacity for sustained scholarly work.

Ibn Hajar studied under dozens of the greatest scholars of his time, traveling to Egypt, the Hijaz, Yemen, and the Levant to seek out the highest authorities in each discipline. In hadith sciences he achieved a mastery that has never been equaled: he studied under scholars with the shortest chains of transmission to the Prophet, memorized tens of thousands of hadiths and their chains, and developed an unparalleled ability to evaluate the reliability of narrators and the authenticity of traditions. He was appointed chief judge (qadi al-qudat) of Egypt, a position he held several times, combining judicial service with his scholarly work.

His bibliography encompasses hundreds of works across all genres of Islamic scholarship. Fath al-Bari, his commentary on Sahih al-Bukhari, is universally recognized as the greatest work of hadith commentary ever written. Tahdhib at-Tahdhib and Taqrib at-Tahdhib are essential references in narrator criticism. Al-Isabah fi Tamyiz as-Sahabah represents his definitive treatment of the Companions of the Prophet. Together these works established him as the supreme authority in hadith sciences.

He died in Cairo in 852 AH (1449 CE), leaving behind a scholarly legacy of unmatched depth and breadth. Ibn Hajar's prolific output — spanning hadith, biography, jurisprudence, Quranic sciences, and history — was sustained over several decades of continuous scholarly labor that his contemporaries marveled at. The combination of extraordinary memory, tireless industry, and precise analytical judgment that made his works so authoritative was recognized in his own lifetime, and the half-millennium since his death has only confirmed what his contemporaries perceived: that no scholar before or since has matched his command of the total hadith tradition.
